metadata.summary: Production guide for calling Anthropic's Claude API directly via SDKs. Covers Messages API, Tool Use, Prompt Caching, Extended Thinking, Streaming, and Batch API with cost optimization techniques.

content.title: Claude API
content.summary: Production guide for calling Anthropic's Claude API directly via the Python and TypeScript SDKs. Covers the Messages API, Tool Use (agentic loops with MAX_TURNS guard), Prompt Caching (90% input-cost reduction on repeated system prompts), Extended Thinking (Opus 4.5 only, minimum 1,024 budget tokens), Streaming (SSE), and the Batch API (50% cost savings, 24-hour window). Always cache system prompts; always enforce MAX_TURNS; always use forced tool use for structured output.

why.p: The Claude API is the lowest-latency path to Claude without proxy overhead. Direct SDK use exposes all billing-reducing features (caching, batching) and all beta capabilities (extended thinking, interleaved thinking with tools) that higher-level frameworks may not surface. For agentic loops where Claude selects tools, direct SDK control over the conversation array is clearer and more debuggable than framework abstractions.

list.item: Building an agentic loop that calls tools and needs full control over the message array
System prompts or large context blocks repeat across requests — Prompt Caching applies immediately
Processing hundreds of documents in batch where 50% cost savings outweigh 24-hour latency
Complex reasoning tasks where Extended Thinking (Opus 4.5) meaningfully improves accuracy
Existing codebase has no multi-provider requirement — LiteLLM abstraction adds unnecessary overhead
The codebase already uses LiteLLM, LangChain, or another multi-provider abstraction — keep that layer
Latency budget is under 200ms — streaming still has SSE overhead; Batch API is unsuitable
Task complexity does not justify Sonnet or above — route to Haiku or a cheaper provider first (see decision-framework)
System is already in production with a working multi-provider setup — don't break it for caching savings

section.name: models
section.summary: Claude model tiers (2025-2026): Haiku 3.5 for speed/cost, Sonnet 4 for balanced production, Opus 4.5 for complex reasoning and Extended Thinking.

list.item: claude-3-5-haiku-20241022 — $0.80/$4.00 per 1M tokens; 200K context; fastest; use for classification, extraction, FAQ
claude-sonnet-4-20250514 — $3.00/$15.00 per 1M tokens; 200K context; default choice for generation, code, RAG
claude-opus-4-5-20251101 — $15.00/$75.00 per 1M tokens; 200K context; Extended Thinking; use for deep reasoning, contract analysis, research synthesis

section.name: messages-api
section.summary: Primary API for all Claude interactions. Single endpoint: POST /v1/messages. Handles single-turn, multi-turn, tool use, vision, and streaming.

rule.statement: Always pass max_tokens explicitly: 512 for Haiku classification, 4096 for Sonnet generation, 8192+ for Opus extended reasoning.
rule.rationale: Default behavior is model-dependent and can result in truncated outputs.

rule.statement: Use the Python SDK (pip install anthropic) or TypeScript SDK (npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk). Do not hand-roll HTTP calls.
rule.rationale: The SDKs handle retries, streaming SSE parsing, and version headers.

rule.statement: Load ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment, never hardcode.
rule.rationale: In production use a secrets manager (1Password, AWS Secrets Manager, Vault).

example.title: Minimal Python call
example.code:
import anthropic client = anthropic.Anthropic() # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env
response = client.messages.create( model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514", max_tokens=1024, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize this document."}], ) print(response.content[0].text)

section.name: tool-use
section.summary: Claude selects and calls tools by returning tool_use blocks. The agent loop appends results as tool_result blocks and continues until stop_reason == "end_turn".

rule.rationale: An agent without it can loop indefinitely when Claude never reaches end_turn due to a misbehaving tool or prompt.

rule.statement: Use forced tool use (tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "output_schema"}) for structured JSON output.
rule.rationale: It is more reliable than prompting for JSON across model updates.
Tool use reliability degrades with large tool sets — curate the tool list per task rather than passing the full registry.

rule.statement: Validate tool inputs against the tool's input_schema before executing.
rule.rationale: Schema drift between Claude's call and the handler's expectations causes silent runtime errors.

example.title: Tool use loop with MAX_TURNS guard

section.name: prompt-caching
section.summary: Marks content blocks with cache_control to reuse them across requests. Cache hit: 90% cost reduction on input tokens. TTL: 5 minutes, extended on each use. Minimum cacheable block: 1,024 tokens.

rule.statement: Always cache system prompts and large static context (retrieved documents, tool schemas) that appear in multiple requests.
rule.rationale: Blocks under 1,024 tokens are silently not cached — do not assume caching on short prompts.

rule.statement: Cache the longest stable prefix first.
rule.rationale: Cached blocks must appear in the same order and at the same position across requests — any change before the cached block invalidates the cache.

rule.statement: Prompt Caching is ineffective for low-traffic applications where the 5-minute TTL expires between requests.
rule.rationale: Profile cache hit rate via usage.cache_read_input_tokens before committing to a caching strategy.

example.title: Cached system prompt with conversation
example.code:
SYSTEM = [ { "type": "text", "text": LARGE_SYSTEM_PROMPT, # must be >= 1024 tokens "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}, } ]
response = client.messages.create( model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514", max_tokens=2048, system=SYSTEM, messages=messages, ) # Check cache hit in usage print(response.usage.cache_read_input_tokens) # > 0 means cache hit print(response.usage.cache_creation_input_tokens) # > 0 means cache write

section.name: extended-thinking
section.summary: Extended Thinking lets Claude reason step-by-step before answering, improving accuracy on math, logic, research synthesis, and code debugging. Available on Opus 4.5 only. Minimum budget: 1,024 tokens.

rule.statement: Start with a small budget (1,024-5,000 tokens). Increase incrementally until accuracy plateaus.
rule.rationale: Thinking tokens are billed at Opus input rate ($15/1M).

rule.statement: Always check for thinking blocks before text blocks when iterating response.content.
rule.rationale: The SDK delivers them as thinking_delta events during streaming — if you only listen for text_delta, you pay for thinking tokens silently.

rule.statement: Do not use Extended Thinking for tasks that need very short outputs (under 200 tokens).
rule.rationale: The minimum 1,024-token budget makes it economically wasteful.

example.title: Extended Thinking call

section.name: streaming
section.summary: Server-sent events (SSE) for real-time token delivery. Python SDK: client.messages.stream() context manager. TypeScript SDK: client.messages.stream(). Event types: message_start, content_block_delta (text_delta / input_json_delta), message_stop.

rule.rationale: Accumulate input_json_delta for tool inputs until the block closes — do not parse partial JSON mid-stream.

rule.statement: On disconnection, resume from the last text block index.
rule.rationale: The SDK does not handle reconnection automatically — track the last delivered text index and skip already-delivered blocks on reconnect.

example.title: Sync streaming with text callback
example.code: with client.messages.stream( model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514", max_tokens=1024, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}], ) as stream: for text in stream.text_stream: print(text, end="", flush=True)

section.name: batch-api
section.summary: Submit up to 10,000 requests as a batch. 50% cost savings vs synchronous. 24-hour processing window. Results via polling or webhook. Use only for offline workloads.

rule.statement: Never route user-facing interactive requests through the Batch API.
rule.rationale: The 24-hour window is incompatible with real-time response expectations.
Offline enrichment (document tagging, summarization, embeddings generation) always goes through Batch. Anything with a user waiting never does.
Check processing_status == "ended" before streaming results. Do not assume completion before polling confirms it.

example.title: Submit and poll a batch

antipattern.name: infinite-tool-loop
antipattern.description: An agent without a MAX_TURNS guard calls tools indefinitely. Claude never reaches end_turn when a tool always returns an error, an ambiguous result, or a prompt that drives Claude to call more tools.
antipattern.reason: Unbounded loops burn tokens at tool_use call rates plus tool result input rates. At Sonnet pricing a 1,000-turn runaway costs ~$5 before hitting rate limits.
antipattern.rule: Always enforce MAX_TURNS = 10 as the default. Log the turn count on every loop iteration. Raise RuntimeError with the loop count when the cap is hit so callers can inspect the conversation array.

antipattern.name: tool-schema-drift
antipattern.description: A tool's input_schema is updated without updating the agent loop handler. Claude generates inputs matching the new schema; the handler expects the old field names and silently ignores or crashes on unknown fields.
antipattern.reason: Schema validation is not automatic — the API passes Claude's tool inputs directly without checking them against the registered schema on the server side.
antipattern.rule: Validate tool inputs against the tool's input_schema (e.g., with jsonschema.validate) before passing them to the handler. Add a test for each tool that asserts the handler accepts the current schema's required fields.

antipattern.name: side-effects-without-checkpoint
antipattern.description: An agentic loop executes irreversible side effects (database mutations, email sends, API POST calls) without a human confirmation step. Claude cannot undo executed side effects.
antipattern.reason: Claude may misinterpret ambiguous instructions or act on stale context, triggering writes the user did not intend.
antipattern.rule: Inject a human-in-loop checkpoint (interrupt_before, confirmation prompt, or dry-run flag) before any tool call with write effects. For automated pipelines, restrict write tools to a sandboxed staging environment until human approval.

antipattern.name: cache-ttl-expiry
antipattern.description: Prompt Caching TTL is 5 minutes. Long gaps between requests invalidate the cache. Applications with fewer than ~12 requests per hour may never benefit from caching.
antipattern.reason: The cache TTL is fixed and not configurable. Low-traffic services pay cache write overhead on every cold request.
antipattern.rule: Profile cache hit rate via usage.cache_read_input_tokens before committing to a caching strategy. If hit rate is below 50%, the caching overhead may not justify the savings.

antipattern.name: cache-prefix-mismatch
antipattern.description: A cached block is prepended with a dynamic prefix (timestamp, request ID, user name) that changes between requests, invalidating the cache on every call.
antipattern.reason: Cache matching is prefix-based. Any content before the cached block that changes between requests breaks the prefix.
antipattern.rule: Place all dynamic content (user message, retrieved context) after the cached block. Static content — system instructions, tool schemas, long documents — must appear before dynamic content in the message array.

antipattern.name: silent-thinking-billing
antipattern.description: Streaming handler only listens for text_delta events. thinking blocks arrive before text blocks as thinking_delta events. The handler discards thinking tokens while still paying for them.
antipattern.reason: Extended Thinking tokens are billed at the full model input rate. Ignoring them in the handler does not skip their billing.
antipattern.rule: Always iterate response.content (or handle thinking_delta in streaming) and extract both block types. Log thinking token count from usage.thinking_tokens to track cost.

antipattern.name: thinking-on-short-output
antipattern.description: Extended Thinking is enabled for a task that requires a short answer (under 200 tokens). The minimum 1,024-token budget makes every call expensive regardless of response length.
antipattern.reason: Budget tokens are always billed even if the model does not use the full budget.
antipattern.rule: Reserve Extended Thinking for tasks where accuracy improvement is measurable: math proofs, multi-step code debugging, research synthesis, contract analysis. Do not apply it to classification, summarization, or FAQ tasks.

section.name: operational-gotchas

antipattern.name: hardcoded-api-key
antipattern.description: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is hardcoded in source code or committed to version control. Key exposure leads to unauthorized usage and billing.
antipattern.reason: API keys in code are frequently leaked via git history, container images, or CI logs.
antipattern.rule: Load keys exclusively from environment variables or a secrets manager. Rotate keys on a schedule. Add ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to .gitignore scanning (e.g., truffleHop, detect-secrets).

antipattern.name: no-retry-on-rate-limit
antipattern.description: Agent makes parallel requests without exponential backoff. HTTP 429 responses crash the loop instead of being retried.
antipattern.reason: Default tier rate limits (requests/minute, tokens/minute) are hit quickly in parallel agentic loops.
antipattern.rule: Implement exponential backoff with jitter: wait = min(2**attempt + random.uniform(0, 1), 60). Use the SDK's built-in retry support or a decorator. Maximum 5 retries before propagating the error.

section.note: Batch API has a 24-hour processing window. If a batch is submitted and polling stops (process crash, restart), retrieve the batch ID from storage and resume polling. Do not resubmit the batch — it results in duplicate processing and double billing.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: An agent calls client.messages.create() with a system prompt (cached), a list of tools, and the current conversation. If Claude returns a tool_use block, the agent executes the tool, appends the result as a tool_result content block, and loops until Claude returns a text block as the final response. For Batch API workloads, the agent writes requests to a JSONL file, submits the batch, polls for completion, and streams results back to the caller.

Recommended subagents: Tool-use orchestrator subagent manages the tool-call loop, handles is_error on tool results, limits retries to 3 before escalating. Batch processor subagent packages tasks into Batch API requests (up to 10,000), polls until done, parses results, returns structured output.

list.item: Always set cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"} on system prompts or large context blocks that appear in multiple requests — 90% input cost reduction on cache hits
Use max_tokens conservatively per model tier: 512 for Haiku classification tasks, 4096 for Sonnet generation, 8192+ only for Opus extended reasoning
Implement exponential backoff with jitter for rate limit errors (HTTP 429): wait = min(2**attempt + random.uniform(0,1), 60)
For structured output, use forced tool use (tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "output"}) rather than prompting for JSON — it's more reliable across model updates
Streaming: process content_block_delta events incrementally; store input_json_delta for tool inputs until input_json is complete; don't buffer full response before acting
Batch API: group requests by deadline — interactive tasks never go in a batch; offline enrichment tasks always should

template.purpose: Cached system prompt + tool-use loop with MAX_TURNS guard
Complete tool-use skeleton with ToolExecutor, retry decorator, forced structured output
